data.title: Sinopharm Group: Mixed Read-Throughs From Peer Disclosures And Management Guidance
data.description:
I continue to have a Neutral opinion of Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:SHTDY) (OTCPK:SHTDF) (1099.HK) or "SGCL".

Its liquidity- and margin-related ratios were analyzed with the previous November 21, 2024 update.

My Hold rating for SHTDF is retained. This considers the unfavorable commentary from other Chinese healthcare distribution companies and SGCL's guide indicative of relative outperformance.

Peers Have A Negative View Of The Sector

Shanghai Pharmaceuticals (OTCPK:SHPMF) (OTCPK:SHPMY) [2607:HK] and China Resources Pharmaceutical (CRPHF) [3320:HK] or "CRP" are distributors of medical products in the PRC ("People's Republic Of China"). They are the main listed comparables for SHTDY.

Their disclosures suggest that the industry is in difficult times.

In 4Q24, SHPMY issued an announcement summarizing its conversations with the buy-side and sell-side. It mentioned the local pharm industry is "under pressure" and "experienced negative growth" last year.

SHPMF subsequently noted at the recent fiscal year earnings call on April 2 that its profitability was hurt by lower drug prices pertaining to Volume Based Procurement ("VBP"). Its FY24 distribution business GPM ("Gross Profit Margin") contracted by -58 bps ("basis points") to 5.9%.

China's latest 10th VBP exercise, whose results will take effect from 2Q25, had over a hundred medicines being priced at less than one-tenth of what they sold for previously.

On the other hand, CRPHF revealed in its investor presentation published in late-March '25 that the company is taking a longer time to collect cash. Its DIO ("Days Inventories Outstanding") metric for the distributor segment was lengthened by six days in 2024. This meant that it needed as long as 3 months to turn sales into cashflow.

At the earlier Morgan Stanley (MS) "China BEST Conference", CRP highlighted that the PRC's "anti-corruption campaign" is now is "more routine" with sales representatives encountering "more restrictions in hospital access." I obtained these management comments from MS's January 9, 2025 research note titled "Conference Feedback" which is only accessible for clients.

I think it's understandable that CRPHF's unsold products are piling up. Chinese hospitals have become more cautious in procuring new medical devices with increased regulatory scrutiny.

SHTDF is dealing with similar problems as its peers, looking at the numbers. Its cash conversion cycle increased by 7 days in FY24. The company suffered from a -0.6 ppts ("percentage points") decline in its gross profit-to-revenue ratio for the prior year.

It's fair to say that the sector is a troubled one, and Sinopharm won't be unaffected.

But SHTDY Could Gain Share Judging By Its Outlook

SGCL welcomed a new president "Mr. Wanyong Lian" at the end of August-2024.

Wanyong knows the company and the pharma-related operations very well. He was with SHTDF between 2009 and 2017. Prior to assuming this new role, Mr Lian was the head of another domestic distributor Shyndec.

Sinopharm reports its financials on an semi-annual basis. Its leader formally communicated with analysts for the first time at the FY2024 results briefing ("transcript not publicly available") on March 24.

Management guided at that event its 2025 topline expansion will be superior to that of the broader sector. There could be a change in focus with the leadership change. In my view, SHTDY has the intention ("implied by guidance") and ability to grab a bigger slice of the domestic medical distribution pie.

Sinopharm, SHPMF, and CRPHF boasted market shares of 23%, 10%, and 8%, respectively. These numbers are sourced from MS' January 5, 2025 report "China Healthcare 2025 Outlook".

The three major players should benefit from sector consolidation, while SHTDF is also likely to win more business than its closest rivals.

This is a fragmented industry with 40 percent of revenue derived from sub-scale entities. These companies' respective revenues are under 1% of the entire market.

Also, SGCL, China Resources, and Shanghai Pharma are all State-Owned Enterprises ("SOEs"). They might have an edge in closing deals with other government healthcare institutions. It could be also easier for them to apply for loans from SOE banks in the country.

My take is that a growing proportion of the industry's sales will be concentrated in the hands of the "Big Three".

Separately, it's highly probable that SHTDY will do better than the company's key competitors for two reasons.

One is that Sinopharm is a pure-play distributor, so its leadership can devote all their attention to the core business. Both CRP and SHPMY have drug development divisions.

The other factor is that SGCL has a scale advantage. All else equal, you would probably have greater confidence in the largest player selling your medical products.

Notwithstanding sector-wide issues detailed in the preceding section, Sinopharm has the potential to achieve financial improvement. This will be realized with share gains and the resulting positive operating leverage effects.

I'm projecting a +4% topline increase and a +10 bps higher net margin for Sinopharm in FY25. This would be a decent set of results in the challenging environment. The company's revenue decreased by 2 percent in 2024, and its net profit-to-sales metric also narrowed by -30 bps during the same period.

The Final Word

I still have a Hold for SHTDY, taking into account industry issues and company-specific merits.

The name is also fairly valued based on a PEG ("Price--To-Earnings Growth") of 1x ("6.6/6.5").

As per S&P Capital IQ data, Sinopharm trades at 6.6 times forward P/E, while its consensus FY24-27 bottomline CAGR estimate is +6.5%.

A peer valuation comparison exercise for SHTDF is inappropriate. I noted above that the other two listed companies have non-distribution operations.

data.title: Micron: Trade War Could Lead To A Stock Collapse
data.description:
Currently, Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) trades below our latest price target of $74.10 per share. That price target was revealed along with the valuation model in our latest article on the company which was published in January. Following the 27% decline of Micron’s stock since January, we believe that the downside could be even greater as the tariff wars made us change some assumptions in the model, which now yields an even lower price target. That is why Micron remains a SELL for us today.

The Trade War Changes Everything

The recent escalation of the global trade war, triggered by new tariff measures imposed by the Trump administration poses a growing threat to Micron’s operation stability. The incoming implementation of tariffs on chips could disrupt the company’s supply chain and affect its performance this year and beyond. Given the company’s reliance on international manufacturing and sales, particularly in Asia, trade policy risks could lead to the contraction of margins in the not-so-distant future.

We also believe that in addition to the potential supply chain issues, Micron’s business could also face indirect effects from tariffs such as frozen procurement cycles, inventory buildup, and risk-off capital rotation. The analysts on Wall Street share a similar view as they outline Micron as one of the companies that are most at risk from tariffs.

Also, Micron’s DRAM and NAND revenues in Q2 were already down 4% Q/Q and 17% Q/Q, respectively. Considering that segments such as consumer, mobile, and automotive memory will likely remain under pressure as a result of the new tariff policy, we believe that it’s a matter of time before orders for chips will decline sharply due to rising costs. This could become a major issue for Micron, which already has a relatively weak gross margin of 34%. Since other semiconductor names like Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) have much better gross margins, it makes sense to believe that Micros is indeed one of the companies that are most at risk from tariffs.

Also, the company still expects to pour around $14 billion into capital expenditures. We view a record CapEx at a time of one of the greatest macroeconomic uncertainties in the last 5 years as a major issue. If the demand for chips falls while costs rise as a result of tariffs, Micron’s ROIC on its record investments might not justify the record spending in the end.

While the company already lost a third of its value in the last six months, we might enter a period of a prolonged downturn as a result of the trade war and the worsening of relations between Beijing and Washington. This could create an additional downside for Micron’s shares and push them to lower prices.

Risk To Our Bearish Thesis

There are still some positive factors that need to be considered. If somehow the trade war deescalates and Washington improves its relations with Beijing, then the aggressive growth of the semiconductor industry, which was predicted in recent months, might resume. This could be a positive thing for Micron since it could lead to the rise in demand for DRAM and NAND memory chips, which are essential to powering large-scale AI workloads. It could also help the company avoid the potential contraction of margins that might happen in the foreseeable future.

The Real Value of Micron

After analyzing the impact that the trade war might have on Micron’s performance, we proceeded to construct a new valuation model to estimate the company’s true worth in the current environment. Our latest model from January which was published before the trade war showed Micron’s intrinsic value to be $74.10 per share. With the number of challenges that the trade war is going to create for Micron, we now believe that the intrinsic value is even lower.

We create the new model when Micron is trading at $69.55 per share. The perpetual growth rate in the model is 3%, which is a rate that we use most of the time when creating valuation models for other businesses. Our projection covers the next five years, and the data like cash and total debt was taken from the most recent earnings report. The discount rate in the model is 7.44%. We calculated it by figuring out Micron’s cost of equity and cost of debt. For the cost of debt calculation, we mostly used its TTM numbers. For the cost of equity calculation, we used a risk-free rate of 4.40%, a beta of 1.18, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

In our forecast table, we expect a gradual decrease in the sales growth rate due to the negative impact of tariffs. That is why in the table the sales forecast for the following years is also lower in comparison to our latest model from January. However, our sales forecast is not that far away from the expectations. While the TTM EBIT margin is 19.5% thanks to the strong performance in the first half of the fiscal year, we expect a contraction of that margin by the end of FY25 and in the upcoming years as well because of the negative impact of the trade war.

The tax rate is 15%, which is the same rate used in the previous article. The CapEx for FY25 is around $14 billion, which is what the management expects this year and what was mentioned earlier in this article. After that, we don’t expect the company to spend over $14 billion annually because of the trade war that will likely affect the economy and make it impossible to justify higher spending. The change in NWC is 1% of sales, which is the same rate we used in our previous model on Micron.

Given these assumptions, our valuation model estimates that Micron’s enterprise value is $71.3 billion. It also shows an equity value of $64.5 billion, which translates to an intrinsic value of $57.45 per share.

Final Thoughts

The trade war has worsened our outlook for Micron. While the company remains a significant player in the y and storage chips market, its performance is likely going to suffer because of the trade war. Given the rise in CapEx, the forecasted contraction of margins, and the normalization of the sales growth rate in the upcoming years, we don’t view Micron as a decent investment right now. Investors may be better served in higher-quality, less cyclical semiconductor names that have less exposure to trade war risks. This is the reason why we still rate Micron as a SELL.

data.title: VåR Energi: Premium Pricing Is Normalizing, I'm At 'BUY'
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Over the past year, I've invested more in oil/energy, specifically European/international oil and energy companies. These companies have included businesses like Eni (E), TotalEnergies (TTE), and others, including some Norwegian local and international businesses. I have positions in both Equinor (EQNR) and Aker BP (OTCQX:AKRBF), a smaller company, but nonetheless interesting that I also follow on Seeking Alpha.

Today, however, I'm going to take a look at one of the arms of Eni S.p.A., the one called Vår Energi (OTCPK:VARRY).

The company has not exactly been a good investment overall over the past few years, owing to a decline of over 30% in that time. However, as the company has been declining in share price has declined, the valuation has obviously grown more attractive.

It's with that in mind that I will be covering the company and its basics here to see what, if any, upside is present in the investment, and why I haven't, as of yet, bought into the company despite a compelling yield and a compelling coverage for that yield.

Vår Energi generates over $2.5B worth of revenue and employs over 1000 people. It's headquartered in attractive Norway, and its latest results, which we'll take a look at here from about 1.5 months ago, are more attractive than the market price would suggest.

Let's look at what we have here.

Vår Energi - Undervalued at almost 9% native yield?

Some basics to begin with that may explain why the company is doing so poorly, comparatively.

The company doesn't hit estimates - it has a 100% failure rate in forecasts, even if those forecast statistics are only 2-3 years of data at this point. The company also has an excessively long-term debt to capital on paper, nearly 99% LT debt/cap, which also colors this company's fundamentals. The company typically pays a very high yield - nearly all of its earnings, and usually more than 100% of its adjusted earnings, go towards paying the dividend (Source: F.A.S.T. Graphs Paywalled Link). However, as I will show you in the article, the company uses different metrics, and there's something to be said for those, given that S&P Global and rating agencies obviously seem to accept them.

That being said, the company is BBB rated (straight BBB) and is backed by some solid finances. It was established in -18 when Eni Norge, a part of the Italian multinational energy company Eni, merged with local Point Resources AS, and the merger created one of the largest independent O&G companies in the entire region, with a production capacity of 300k/mbbl per day.

Furthermore, Vår Energi has not been idle. They bought Exxon Mobil's (XOM) upstream assets, all of them, in the NCF for a total of $4.5B, and this included interests in a significant number of producing fields on the NCF, including attractive Grane, Fram, and Snorre.

This has made Vår Energi AS the second-largest operator after Equinor, even larger than Aker BP.

So, Vår Energi, despite being treated as small, is not small. Its objectives are the exploration and production of oil and natgas on the NCF, the so-called Norwegian Continental Shelf. It has interests in both operating and non-operating fields at this time.

It should come as no surprise to you that the company's primary correlation is the oil and gas price, bar none. The base case for more oil and gas is good.

NCS is attractive. Why is NCS attractive? Because NCS production is cheaper, with lower emissions, and has a lot of resources. Furthermore, it's close to Norway, one of the richest countries per capita in the entire world.

While the current geopolitical climate does a good job of "coloring" this outlook, I have never lost sight of the fact that this is a very attractive source for European energy - and neither has Norway or Europe, looking at the size of export and the support given. That is also why significant capital of mine is invested in companies operating here.

Vår Energi has a solid record of delivering growth - not only in production, up from 169 kboepd in 169, to 280 in 2024 compared to 2018, with reserves up from 900 billion boe to 1.2 billion, but also the important ROACE, up from 16% to 20% in 2024 since the company's founding.

The company uses EBITDAX as a metric, which is somewhat unorthodox - it's essentially earnings before interest, tax, D&A and exploration expenses, somewhat questionable but also logical for a company like this - and the annual EBITDAX has risen from $2.7B in 2018, to almost $6B in 2024 - and the company isn't using $3B a year in exploration expenses (Source: Vår Energi IR)

I therefore say that Vår Energi is creating value, and you can see how here.

I believe that in this increasingly uncertain geopolitical world, it will pay off to own local producers of energy, and this is one of them for me, where I live. It would be the same logic as owning local producers in the US if I lived there.

Furthermore, as EU reliance on Russian gas has lowered, and I view it questionable if Nord Stream is ever being restarted, the company's gas position is getting more and more important as well.

So, the basics for this company are solid, I will argue. The company's 2024 delivered strong results, as most EU-based oil and gas companies did, with increased production, growing reserves, and reduced unit OpEx. That unit OpEx is one of the key ratios you want to look at. It means that Vår Energi could essentially "survive" with prices below $15/bbl net of logistical costs, at a unit OpEx of $12.8/boe, which is better than guidance. The company's official breakeven is at about $35/boe, which is only beaten by some Arabic and African producers, as well as some Russian ones. Not many others in Europe can produce at this cost/rate.

Several large key projects are nearing completion as well, and the company's discovery rates have been good, at 50%, adding almost 85 mmboe to the company. The company has responded by paying 30% of its post-tax CFFO in dividends for the year, and for 2025E it already has very lofty targets, including a dividend raise.

The company is targeting an FCF potential of $5-$9B between 2025-2030, of which 25-30% will be returned to shareholders, while becoming Co-neutral in 2030.

Those are some impressive positives, aren't they?

So, where are the risks?

Near-term or fundamental liquidity isn't necessarily an issue, despite some of the leverage ratios from some perspectives. The company has an average debt maturity of over 5 years and $1.3B in available liquidity, which is enough to fund operations well. To counter the leverage, the company is calculating a leverage ratio of 0.8x at year-end 2024, based on net-interest-bearing debt, including leases on an R12 EBITDAX. The danger here is that this ratio could obviously take hits from unsuccessful exploration and the like - still, fundamentals as I see them are quite solid.

The main risks to Vår Energi AS, given the mergers and the size and scale advantages the company now operates at, are, in my view, limited to the main correlations. As we've seen with the recent geopolitical trends, the company's share price has gone down in conjunction with the oil price and with the gas price uncertainty. The forecasts we have for the company, which I'll get into, almost can't be argued to be forecasts to heed at a 100% failure rate.

The uncertainty is, therefore, the main problem - and it's this that we must price for.

So, let's price it.

Vår Energi Valuation and upside - here, we can invest.

One of the trickiest questions I considered when looking at Vår Energi was whether to consider the company speculative, not because of its size, but because of end markets and correlations. In the end, I chose not to do so. The size and the breadth of its operations would make such a classification unfair.

But I want to emphasize that Vår Energi is quite volatile, as we've seen in the past 3 years. The current upside is very exuberant if you were to listen to the forecasts and where the company typically trades. Even taking the lowest historical P/E rating and discounting it further by 10-20% brings us to a P/E of 13.5x on a forward basis, and even there, we see a 180 %+ total RoR potential to 2027E.

The native yield of this company is more than 16% at this time. Whenever you encounter a yield this size, there are risks involved - significant ones. No different for this one. If the oil price were to continue to drop, this company's profits could be "carved into", and that dividend would quickly need to be cut, which likely would result in further share price decline. So, the forecast you see above, while not speculative, I would consider to be "optimistic" at the very least.

On the other hand, valuation.

Vår Energi is currently priced at only 0.76x sales. The more assets the company has and the longer the company has been trading like this, the lower those multiples will be. 1.69x EV/EBITDA, with a levered FCF yield of close to 30%.

Closest international and local competition? Vår Energi can be compared to Occidental (OXY), and Vår Energi is priced at half the multiples of OXY in terms of revenue and less than a quarter in terms of EBITDA. This company is cheap both in terms of forward multiples and in terms of peers; there's no doubt in my mind about that. (Source: Paywalled Tikr.com link)

It is, in fact, even cheaper priced than Aker BP at this time, another company I invest in. Street Target confirms this. 14 analysts follow Vår Energi, and they average at around 35-36 NOK/share, with 11 out of 14 analysts at a "Buy" or "Outperform" rating. The company isn't as cheap as it was in September, in terms of Price/NAV (currently 1.02x), but it's cheaper than it was only 1-2 months ago.

I believe the time has now come to "BUY" Vår Energi AS - and I am taking the plunge. As of this morning, I am adding 0.1% to my portfolio in Vår Energi AS for the long term, and I am giving the company a long-term PT of buying below 36 NOK for the native for an upside to an 8-9x 2026-2027 P/E with a possible FV as high as 60 NOK (But again, very optimistic).

The company's ADR is called VARRY, and it's a 2x ADR, which means that 1 VARRY equals 2 native shares of VAR. That means my 36 NOK price target comes to a VARRY target of $6.63/share. However, given the thin trading of the company's ADR, I would only consider the native.

My introductory thesis for Vår Energi is as follows.

Thesis

  • Vår Energi is a fundamentally attractive oil & gas company. With the current geopolitical uncertainties, I have been expecting declines in local companies in the EU, Asia, Africa, and other areas, and I have not been disappointed in what has been happening.
  • Vår Energi has now declined to below 29 NOK/share for the native ticker, and in any oil/gas or forecast case, this marks an attractive thesis and upside for me. I therefore rate this company as a "BUY" at this time.
  • The forecasts have now been, in my view, adjusted down to give an upside even in the case of very conservative scenarios. I forecast Vår Energi at a 13x forward P/E, and based on the company's forward earnings in 2026-2027, this comes to an implied share price of over 60 NOK for the native. I give the company a PT of 36 NOK or below, where it is an attractive buy, taking into consideration potential downsides in oil price and geopolitical trends.
  • This company has a triple-digit potential upside.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of 36 NOK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Uber Technologies: I'm Bearish Due To Weakening Consumer Spending
data.description:
Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) has delivered a compelling set of results in recent years. The company had generated large operating losses for most of the past decade, and some investors questioned whether it would ever evolve into a strongly profitably enterprise.

However, Uber has taken advantage of its scale benefits, extending its market share lead in rideshare in many markets. Meanwhile, growth efforts in fields such as food delivery have also paid off. Uber is now delivering solid profits and cash flows. And, given its market share lead, some analysts see it having a path to above-average returns for many years to come.

With the stock consolidating between $60 and $80 for the past year now, are shares more likely to head upward or downward out of this range going forward? While the company's recent results are laudable, I'm bearish on UBER stock in the bigger picture.

For one, Uber shares dropped from $60 to $20 during the previous bear market. This is a stock that is highly sensitive to economic conditions and market sentiment. With the uncertainty around tariffs, inflation, and an apparent slowdown in consumer spending, macro conditions are a clear headwind for Uber Technologies in 2025. There are more than just near-term concerns to keep in mind, however.

No Guarantee Of Long-Term Dominance

If Uber were assured of being a monopoly or at least dominant player within its competitive space for many years to come, it'd be easier to underwrite the current valuation.

However, technology continues to evolve rapidly. And there are still numerous competitors to Uber in both the ride hailing and e-convenience areas.

In the near term, companies like Lyft (LYFT) and DoorDash (DASH) can compete aggressively with Uber on price, keeping margins from growing. That's especially likely in a recession, when these firms will probably turn to discounting to maintain rider and delivery volumes.

Arguably, Uber's biggest competitive edge to-date has been its larger pool of drivers and broader geographic reach, particularly in overseas markets. Uber will need to maintain solid ridership levels to keep drivers satisfied and sufficiently compensated during a potential recession scenario, and that likely implies a hit to margins.

And then, in the longer-term, Uber's leadership in terms of having the largest driver pool may not end up being nearly as valuable. Uber is a classic network effects story. More drivers mean that customers get faster fulfillment on their requests. This creates greater customer loyalty. It's been a virtuous cycle so far.

But in a world where human drivers are not the key limiting factor to fulfilling rider requests, I'm not certain Uber's competitive advantage holds. Rather, I tend to agree with fellow Seeking Alpha author Julian Lin, who argued that Uber Has A Waymo Problem. In that piece, Lin wrote that:

"As Waymo improves its production capabilities, I would expect the autonomous costs to go down, and for the ROI to go up. While Waymo does not have the same supply as Uber today, this is looking like a situation in which it is only a matter of time before Waymo makes the decision to invest heavily in ramping up its fleet. I view Waymo as being similar to Amazon (AMZN) many decades ago when it was building out its logistics infrastructure at high ROI. [Alphabet], with over $100 billion in annual operating income, could theoretically build up a fleet comparable to Uber within a handful of years, if not sooner."

Lin raises a good point in suggesting that a big tech firm with a fortress balance sheet could easily deploy capital at great scale here and disrupt the existing players like Uber and DoorDash. Will it happen? It's hard to say at this point.

We can imagine a situation where the autonomous vehicle developers end up partnering with Uber rather than trying to take the whole pie for themselves. It's also notable that Uber has a large presence in lower-income emerging markets. It's probable that autonomous vehicles will take longer to supplant human drivers in markets where the cost of labor is lower.

Still, autonomous vehicles are a huge, potentially even existential, risk to companies like Uber and Lyft. I'm cautious to assign too large of a valuation multiple to Uber, given the dramatic range of uncertainty around long-term terminal valuation of these businesses.

2024 Earnings Benefitted From A Tax Item

Some investors have seen that UBER stock is trading at 16x reported trailing earnings and believe that to be a discount. 16x earnings for a tech company with double-digit growth certainly sounds interesting at first glance. And, technically speaking, that is true enough:

However, as the table shows, the company's forward P/E ratio (for FY'26) is closer to 23x rather than 16x, which implies a substantial drop in earnings for both this year and next year compared to 2024's results. Similarly, the company's other metrics such as more than 7x book value, 3.5x sales, and 44x EV/EBITDA do not scream obvious bargain either.

The issue that distorts the trailing P/E figure is that the company reported a $6.4B tax valuation adjustment in its favor in Q4 2024. All told, Uber reported $6.9 billion in Q4 earnings, but the majority of that was from the tax matter rather than the core business.

For full-year 2024, Uber Technologies reported just $2.8 billion in operating profit. However, it enjoyed a $1.3 billion benefit from net interest income along with other non-operating income. And then it had a total benefit of $5.8 billion from tax, causing its overall 2024 profitability to swell to $9.9 billion.

If you just take the company's actual operating income of $2.8 billion for 2024, however, then Uber would be trading at more than a 50x multiple to that underlying earnings power.

Analysts are estimating $2.42 of EPS for FY'25, which would put the stock around 30x this year's expected earnings:

Given that the company has about 2.1 billion shares of stock outstanding, this implies roughly $5.1 billion in net income for this year.

For reference, the company generated just $2.8 billion in operating income last year, along with about $200 million in net interest income. I'm not convinced that the company will jump from around $3 billion in core earnings power last year to more than $5 billion this year, given the macroeconomic outlook. Of course, Uber could benefit once again from non-operating income, tax effects or whatnot, but on a core earnings basis, this year's $2.42 estimate seems ambitious.

Analysts then see EPS continuing to surge higher, rising 36% in 2026 and 26% in 2027. Uber is supposed to accomplish this off of just 14% annualized revenue growth. It's not impossible to grow EPS at a far faster rate than revenues, of course. But it'd be a tough ask if anything happens that impacts profit margins. My view is that there will be more competition on pricing to maintain ridership levels in a potential recession scenario. Also, I expect consumer discretionary income to be pressured due to price increases tied to tariffs, which will further challenge the rideshare space and potentially force more discounting. As a final point, the tariffs could lead to significantly higher auto-prices which, in turn, could force drivers to demand pay increases to offset increased depreciation and operating costs for their vehicles.

UBER Stock Verdict

Some investors might look to Uber and other rideshare companies as a relative safe haven during the current tariff-driven economic uncertainty. It's true that Uber isn't directly reliant on imported goods from China, meaning it has less outright exposure than, say, many retailers and e-commerce companies.

However, I believe consumer spending will be significantly pressured in a scenario where the current tariffs persist for any meaningful length of time. Specifically, restaurant prices are likely to rise given the prevalence of imported produce, packaging materials, equipment, etc. that go into restaurant products, which could pressure margins and reduce demand for food delivery services. And, overall, consumers are likely to pull back on discretionary spending, which could mean fewer nights out, rides to the airport, and so on.

Combined with the longer-term uncertainty around autonomous vehicles and where Uber fits within that ecosystem, I see both short- and long-term challenges which could impact valuation. And shares aren't nearly as cheap as they look based merely on trailing 2024 earnings. All this makes UBER stock a sell right now.

data.title: Could A Recession Accelerate AI Adoption And Turn Palantir To A Defensive Play?
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) is hardly the ticker that jumps to mind when thinking about defensive stocks. Even after a 25%+ correction from All-Time-Highs, the company still trades at a P/E ratio of above 160 and with a market cap of ~70 times its 2024 revenue.

Today, ahead of Palantir’s Q1 Earnings in early May (Monday, May 5th), I want to present a somewhat provocative idea: that Palantir could behave like a defensive stock in case of a “Trumpcession” - which I define as a willingly induced economic recession with non-zero risk of inflation remaining sticky.

I think a “Trumpcession” could drive AI adoption, much like the 2020 pandemic boosted eCommerce, EVs, remote work, and other trends. I believe Palantir may benefit disproportionately from this shift, similar to how pharmaceutical and eCommerce companies thrived in 2020.

The case for Palantir as a “defensive play”

There is no denying that Palantir’s valuation is very rich. No matter the company's long term investment thesis (more of my thoughts on this later), readers may still wonder whether it makes sense to wait for a “correction” to build a position in this stock.

Today, I make the case that such a correction may never manifest, even (or, especially) in the case of a “Trumpcession.”

I would expect a U.S. and/or global recession to have the following macro effects:

  1. A cooldown in consumer spending

  2. Pressure on companies’ top and bottom lines, resulting in layoffs and a rise in unemployment

  3. A significant increase in government spending, both to prop up the economy and to provide some social shield to the unemployed masses.

These effects may be compounded if inflation remains sticky - something I covered in my recent work analyzing President Trump’s economic policy.

I will now share three reasons why I believe Palantir’s business is highly resilient - or even strengthened - by the effects of a recession I outlined above.

For each of these reasons, I will provide tangible examples to show why Palantir can thrive in such macro conditions. I will list these reasons from the last to the most important in the context of my thesis.

Palantir is well aligned with U.S. governments, no matter the color

The first element of strength I see may be the most obvious: Palantir is a company that was very aligned with the Biden administration and seems to be very aligned with the Trump administration, too.

Despite his CEO reportedly backing Democrats in the 2024 election, Palantir has very close ties with many in Trump's administration. The company published public advice in matters of AI to the current administration in March, stating that “The United States must act decisively to maintain its AI leadership position.” One of its co-founders, Peter Thiel, is also considered one of the closest people to Trump and his advisors.

Perhaps the elements that most point at how close Palantir is with the current U.S. administration are its deals and product offering. Palantir has been reported to be helping DOGE with a “massive” IRS project, using its software to become the “read center of all IRS systems.”

The company has also started marketing itself as a partner that can help navigate trade tariffs, reporting six new commercial deals with companies to “re-industrialize American manufacturing” and “accelerate on-shore manufacturing capabilities.”

Finally, It is very recent news that Palantir is partnering with SpaceX (SPACE) and Anduril to collaborate on Trump’s “Golden Dome” project.

What is even more important in my view is that Palantir seems to be “administration neutral.” The company reported a deal with the Pentagon one year ago, when the U.S. administration was in the hands of President Joe Biden. This was centered around “Maven,” a military software platform designed to support military operations. It integrates and analyzes data from diverse sources, such as satellites and social media, to provide real-time intelligence.

Maven is a software solution that is rapidly proving to be highly valuable, having been extended to a full partnership with NATO just recently (more on this shortly). Palantir also offers “FedStart,” a SaaS solution the company describes as targeting “startups looking to deploy software to the federal government.”

In my view, this shows that Palantir is a non-political organization that is solely focused on delivering value to U.S. administrations, no matter its color.

Palantir’s core business is unlikely to be affected by tariffs or retaliation

As a software company, Palantir’s core business is not directly affected by the trade war that Trump has started in his first 100 days in office. Tariffs - at least so far - are only covering goods, not services. And that, I believe, is by design. When accounting for services, the U.S. is actually running a relatively small trade deficit with the rest of the world.

Of course, trade partners may decide to hit back at the U.S. by targeting its service industry. While that has not materialized so far, some EU members have signaled they would like to do so. That may come in the form of ad-hoc legislation targeting U.S. tech companies, or the closing of legal tax loops that have allowed Tech companies to optimize their tax bills in Europe for a long time. What makes potential retaliation especially dangerous, in my opinion, is the fact that the U.S. Tech sector is in the hands of a few big players. This makes it relatively easy for nation-states or the EU to tune any law to disproportionately affect these names.

What I find interesting in this context is that Palantir should be better shielded from potential retaliation, for two reasons:

  1. Palantir is not a member of the “Magnificent 7” and it is still a relatively small player in the U.S. tech space, at a market cap of just above $ 200 Bln. I believe ad-hoc “retaliatory” legislation is unlikely to hit Palantir, as it’s equally unlikely to hit companies such as Salesforce, Inc. (CRM).

  2. More importantly, Palantir makes most of its money in the USA, which is also where most of its growth came from in Q4. Its exposure to markets outside of the U.S. and Europe is insignificant. Any counter measures aimed at U.S. tech companies paying more taxes abroad would have little to no impact on Palantir.

Finally, there is also a political aspect to consider. I think the recent NATO deal with Palantir on Maven shows that the company is deeply entrenched in Western politics.

While NATO itself has been put under questioning by the Trump administration, Palantir is also active in war theaters in Ukraine and in Israel, and it maintains a strong, pro-Western political stance. In the words of its CEO, Alex Karp, Palantir: “power(s) the West to its [...] innate superiority”.

Interestingly in my view, Palantir has also announced a deal with the French bank Société Générale Société anonyme (OTCPK:SCGLF), aimed at reducing fraud risks. This is a bank that is considered of systemic importance for France and Europe, and has ties with the current French government, which has been one of the advocates for retaliation against America.

Ultimately, I believe a company that is actively involved in two active war conflicts and entrenched with Western military politics is, if anything, more likely to be excluded from retaliation by the EU and Western allies such as Japan or South Korea.

Palantir’s commercial business is positioned to benefit from a recession

As I have outlined at the beginning of this section, I would expect the first macro element of a recession to be a reduction in consumer spending. This, in turn, would push companies to pursue cost cutting measures to protect their margins, including layoffs.

I find it very likely that in the current day and age, a company looking at cost cutting measures would first of all try to see whether AI can be of help.

Since the launch of AIP and its focus on U.S. commercial customers, Palantir has reported a plethora of deals with corporate entities that have resulted in significant cost cutting measures. Its last AIPCon, a conference the company routinely organizes to showcase customers’ results, has shown a fair amount. Just to mention a few that I personally find particularly interesting:

  • A deal with Walgreens Boots Alliance, Inc. (WBA), boosting efficiency in onboarding in-store operation by 30%

  • A deal with AT&T (T), cutting unnecessary dispatches by 40%

  • A deal with Heineken N.V. (OTCQX:HEINY), improving global supply chain logistics

Beyond existing deals, I find myself once again pointing at the picture above, which Nvidia Corporation's (NVDA) CEO used a few months ago to describe the advancement of AI.

In a nutshell, I believe that we are on the verge of the switch between Generative AI and Agentic AI. The Agentic AI “revolution” will see applications that may be able to substitute white collar workers and disrupt the job market for good - something I covered in depth in my last piece on Palantir.

Palantir, in my view, is positioned to benefit enormously from the switch to Agentic AI, as the leading global company that is able to build on the “application layer” of Generative AI. In the context of a recession, I see Palantir as a strong player that can build Agentic AI applications to substitute human labor for its clients. That is especially interesting when considering how Palantir already has a step in many corporations thanks to its existing work and ramp up of the US Commercial business.

In this regard, I believe that a U.S. and/or global recession may do for AI what the 2020 pandemic has done for eCommerce, EVs and other industries connected with remote working: acting like a catalyst. Companies may be forced to accelerate their AI adoption to cut costs and allow for more layoffs. Palantir -as one the leading providers for such technology- would stand to benefit.

Palantir: sticking to my $ 250 Long Term Price target

I have covered Palantir on Seeking Alpha since May 2024, when the company traded at ~$24 per share. I had an initial price target of $125 that I raised in January to $ 250 per share, assuming the company will experience accelerated growth at a CAGR of ~30%+ per year.

Palantir has surprised me in that the market realized very quickly its potential, making its valuation grow far beyond the results that could be materially delivered in just one year's time. There is no doubt in my mind that today the company is richly valued. As a result, high volatility may still manifest in the upcoming weeks (something I will cover shortly in the risk section of this article).

However, I focus on the long term and I only invest in single stocks if I am willing to hold them for at least 10 years. Palantir builds AI software applications that deliver tangible results, and have a direct impact on governments and businesses. Palantir’s capabilities have been shown not only by financial results (Palantir boasts a Rule of 40 of 81 as of Q4), but also by a series of high profile deals the company keeps reporting. This is why I remain fundamentally bullish long term, seeing the potential for this company to grow significantly from here.

I invite readers that are interested in understanding in depth the bull case of Palantir to read my previous coverage on the stock, and especially my last article where I outline how the company fits the AI adoption curve.

Risks to my thesis: do not buy PLTR to limit volatility

The main risk with Palantir possibly behaving as a defensive stock in the context of a recession concerns its valuation. With such a rich valuation, even the slightest sign of weakness in terms of guidance or financial results may act like a negative catalyst and trigger a significant stock correction. That may happen regardless of a recession manifesting, at any point in time.

With this article, I am in no way suggesting readers to actually buy Palantir purely as a defensive, short term play in the context of a possible recession. This article is a somewhat provocative piece that, I hope, will offer readers a different outlook on Palantir’s core business and how it may behave in the upcoming months.

Similarly, as obvious as it may sound, I strongly encourage readers to refrain from buying Palantir with the explicit purpose of limiting the volatility of their portfolio. I believe investors should consider starting a position in Palantir (or adding to one) only with a long term investment horizon.

Further risks concern the broader AI space. While I believe we are nowhere near the “end” of the AI Hype, a recession may trigger a cooldown in some investments in AI and that may deflate valuations in the Tech space and impact Palantir as well.

Conclusion

I believe Palantir Technologies could emerge as a surprisingly resilient, even defensive, play in the face of a potential “Trumpcession.”

I think its deep alignment with U.S. government priorities, insulation from trade war impacts, and ability to drive AI-driven cost efficiencies for businesses position it to thrive amid economic turbulence.

While its rich valuation invites volatility and demands a long-term perspective, I remain bullish on Palantir’s potential to capitalize on accelerated AI adoption.

I am sticking to my $250 price target as the company continues to deliver tangible value to governments and corporations alike, and rate the company a STRONG BUY. On a personal note, I am long 1,500 shares and plan to keep holding, potentially adding to my position in case of price corrections.

data.title: Edison International: Earn Up To 6% Yield From The Top Utility Pick Until It Gets Spicy
data.description:
Amid blazing global trade disputes, the utilities sector is shaping up to be a good alternative for investors to endure the current macro uncertainty and market volatility. My recent article on Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) highlighted the strong fundamentals for utility players in light of the expected energy consumption and capital investment projects. A year prior to this, I had a neutral position on the utility stocks, which were overshadowed by the solid gains in the technology sector. However, the rapid diffusion of data center systems secured strong foundations for power demand growth.

Against the backdrop of rising recession fears, the utility stocks offer an attractive dividend yield, while in the context of tariff-induced market volatility, the sector is shining in the green territory, proving to be a safe haven so far. As a result, in this piece, I decided to take a look at XLU holdings in more detail in order to determine the most attractive positions in the fund. I come up with the following scoring approach, which ranks the companies based on the analysis of the key financial fundamentals.

*The table lists only the top 10 companies out of 31 overall in the score list. The lower the final score, the higher the company is ranked in the selection according to the specific metric. If you want additional information regarding the score of the stocks in the Utility Select Sector not included in the table, feel free to ask me in the comment section.

The final score of a company is determined by weighting the dividend yield (20% weight), FCF margin (20% weight), net debt to EBITDA (20% weight), long-term growth forecast (10% weight), 12-month potential (10% weight), and valuation by P/E ratio (20% weight). All the metrics in the table are based on forward analysts’ estimates apart from ND/EBITDA and CFO/Capex, which are estimated with trailing data.

As a result, the Edison International (NYSE:EIX) stock exerts the most attractive score. The company is going through a regulatory investigation related to the Eaton fire, aimed at determining the implication of the utility infrastructure in the cause of the blaze. Let’s dive into it with more details.

Edison International: a hot pick in the utility sector

Edison International is a Californian electric power holding company whose key division is Southern California Edison (“SCE”), primarily engaged in the distribution and sale of electricity in Southern California. SCE is currently under scrutiny following the Eaton fire at the beginning of the year. The utilities equipment is alleged to be involved in sparking the blaze due to the faulty power lines.

The market reaction followed suit, with EIX shares down nearly 30% year to date. However, the scrutiny for determining the official cause of the fire could prove to be quite lengthy, where the executives mentioned that it could take 12 to 18 months even to get an investigation report from official fire authorities.

Meanwhile, the regulatory environment in California remains favorable for EIX. The company has received approval for a $1.6 billion reimbursement for costs related to the 2017/2018 fires, which will have a material positive impact on 2025 financials. In addition, the utility reached substantial completion of resolving claims for Woolsey and filed the cost recovery application.

Turning to the financial results, the company demonstrated a strong and resilient path of meeting earnings guidance over the past 2 decades. The FY2024 core EPS came above the guidance midpoint, up to $4.93, compared to $4.76 per share a year ago.

Looking forward, the SCE capital plan is focused on replacing aging infrastructure, continuing grid hardening, and expanding investments. EIX maintains its investment plan, where capital deployment is expected to increase significantly in the 2025-2028 period. Up to 85% of the investments are dedicated to the company’s distribution grid in order to support wildfire mitigation strategy and clean energy transformation.

The implementation of this program could secure long-term financial growth for the utility, as the regulated asset base is expected to increase by 9% on average over the following 4 years. This gives management confidence for up to 7% earnings growth through 2028, up from $5.84 EPS forecasted in 2025 at midpoint.

The resilient financial results for 2024 allowed for a 6.1% raise in the quarterly dividend, up to $0.83, achieving a strong pattern of 21 consecutive annual increases. In particular, the dividend policy is the area where SCE stands out compared to the utility selection mentioned earlier. With a 5.8% forward dividend yield, the company ranks #2 on the list, only falling short of AES Corporation with a 6.9% expected yield.

As you might notice, it’s not just the dividend yield that secures the best rank for EIX; it’s about the fundamentals that stand behind it. The company is expected to generate a solid free cash flow with an FCF margin of 7.2%, ranking #5 according to this measure. Strong FCF provides a good indication of a company’s capability to pay and potentially increase dividends without compromising operations or growth initiatives. As a result, the question is whether this profitability would be enough. The guidance stipulates up to 20% YoY earnings growth in 2025, where the FCF yield is anticipated to cover the dividend yield. At the same time, EIX operates at reasonable debt levels, as the company ranks just in the middle of the selection by ND/EBITDA ratio. Not surprisingly, the management raised the quarterly dividend distribution and manifested confidence in the continuation of the policy. The latter is underpinned by the solid long-term growth prospects secured by the solid capital investment program, which classifies EIX at #7 here. Moreover, the market sentiment seemed to be moderately positive, as the analyst estimated a 17% potential gain over the 12-month horizon. This could be related to the timing of the ongoing scrutiny. What is interesting is that the short interest for EIX stock is just below 2%, which is one of the lowest among the utility players.

No doubt, the concerns about the Eaton fire consequences remain elevated. Still, the executives are confident in the California wildfire fund, as the SCE has received timely approval of its safety certification each year, providing a presumption of prudence and a cap on the liability to reimburse the wildfire fund. SCE continues to be committed to the wildfire mitigation processes by expanding its covered conductor deployment up to 6 400+ miles. The AB 1054 framework allows California-based IOUs to tap into a $21 billion fund if their equipment is found to be responsible for a blaze and the utility company acted responsibly. Although SCE would draw from its $1 billion self-insurance fund before addressing the state fund. In case utility actions are found to be imprudent, the liability cap for SCE to reimburse the fund is capped at $3.9 billion.

Ultimately, all this comes at a reasonable valuation. The forward P/E ratio of EIX stands at 9.5x, which is the second lowest and is considerably lower in relation to the average ratio of 19.1x in the selection. In addition, the company is currently trading at circa 30% discount to its 3Y median P/E of 13.7x.

Investment conclusion

EIX is currently bundled with the risk concerning the Eaton blaze. The consequences for the utility and the timing of the regulatory investigations remain unclear. Meanwhile, the company is proving to be the hottest pick, according to my approach, which indicates that Edison International provides the best combination of key fundamentals. Namely, the company offers a high dividend yield, backed by a strong cash flow generation potential, which is expected to sustain the future distributions without resorting to leverage. EIX commands a relatively low debt level, which is particularly prudent during economic uncertainty and high interest rate environment. The management is confident in the company’s regulatory proceedings, maintaining the strong capital investment program, which secures attractive long-term growth prospects. The market participants, in turn, remain moderately positive regarding the stock’s potential due to the valid AB 1054 legislation and an appealing valuation, which makes me bullish on EIX as well.

data.title: SmartCentres Offers A Walmart-Backed High Dividend Yield
data.description:
Introduction

A few years ago, I quite liked the relationship between SmartCentres (OTCPK:CWYUF) ( SRU.UN:CA) and its largest tenant, Walmart (WMT). Walmart represents in excess of 23% of SmartCentres’ rental revenue, with other grocery-related tenants representing an additional 7% of the rental income. Throwing in some other high-traffic stores like a Canadian Tire, Winners and Dollarama, and about 40% of the total rental revenue is generated from what I would consider to be reliable tenants.

Looking back at the AFFO performance

One of the elements I appreciate most about Canadian REITs is the fact that the AFFO calculation includes the sustaining capex required to keep the properties in good shape. Before discussing the AFFO result, it makes sense to have a look at the FFO first. As you can see below, the REIT had a pretty strong final quarter of 2024 wherein it reported a total FFO of C$101.4M after making some adjustments. That being said, if we would just look at the normalized FFO before taking certain non-recurring elements into account, we see the FFO decreased by almost 10% to just C$96.6M.

That’s a pity as the first few quarters of the year were quite strong, and on a full-year basis, the REIT’s FFO (before adjustments) increased by just under 0.5% to C$402.5M. The main reason for this can be found in the cost of debt. Whereas SmartCentres recorded a net interest expense of C$144M in FY 2023, this increased to C$169M in FY 2024. The weighted average interest rate on the secured debt was 3.97% while the average cost of the unsecured debt was 3.84%.

The weaker FFO on a reported basis also had a negative impact on the AFFO result. As you can see below, the AFFO decreased from C$92.2M to C$85M in the final quarter of 2024, while the full-year AFFO increased by almost 1.5% to C$359.4M.

The AFFO adjusted for the elements shown above was slightly lower on a full-year basis (but higher in the final quarter of last year). SmartCentres currently pays a monthly distribution of C$0.1542, and with a full-year AFFO of C$2.02 and C$1.91 per share after taking some of the adjustments into consideration, the distribution is covered, although the payout ratio remains high.

At the end of 2024, the book value per share was just over C$30, underpinned by the real estate assets which were valued at a discount rate of 6.53%. Also, keep in mind, the balance sheet contains approximately C$1.72B in properties under development, which of course are not yet contributing to the rental income.

Where will growth come from?

As the most recent debt issue by SmartCentres was a 6.5-year unsecured note with a 4.737% coupon, I think we should expect the total net interest expenses to increase further as cheaper debt securities will have to be refinanced with more expensive capital as SmartCentres is still working through its debt refinancing cycle. The proceeds of the new unsecured note were used to repay a C$160M 3.556% unsecured debt security, while later this year a 1.74% C$350M unsecured debt security will have to be refinanced. Adding 250-300 bps to the cost of debt to refinance the latter will likely cause the interest expenses to increase by approximately C$10M. Applying an average cost of debt of 4.25% to the C$5B in gross debt would result in C$212M in interest expenses, resulting in an increase of approximately C$50M per year.

This will, of course, weigh on SmartCentres’ financial results, but there are three reasons why I think the REIT should be able to fully absorb the higher interest expenses.

First of all, it will be able to hike the rent its tenants pay based on the inflation rate. Assuming an average increase in the net rental income by 2% per year would increase the NOI by C$11M per year. This means that, based on the current asset base, three years further down the road, about C$33M of the anticipated C$50M interest expense increase will be covered by (relatively low) rent hikes.

Secondly, the company is working on the completion of its development portfolio. As you can see below, the REIT should deliver some growth in its self-storage division, followed by the completion of residential real estate in 2027.

This will allow it to generate additional income and/or recycle some of its capital investments in the development space.

And finally, it’s perhaps important to emphasize about 69% of its portfolio is up for re-leasing in the next five years. As you can see below, SmartCentres has highlighted the current average market rent is approximately $29.9 per square foot versus the $23.48 per square foot in the expiring contracts.

A lot will depend on the negotiations, and I’m not convinced we can apply a 1:1 rent increase as SmartCentres may have to give some tenants a break in order to keep the traffic high and occupancy ratio robust, but even a $2.5/sq.ft. rent increase across the portfolio would result in a total additional rental income of around C$50M.

Additionally, some vacant properties were recently re-leased to new tenants.

Investment thesis

So while the higher interest rates will definitely weigh on the REIT’s financial results, I'm quite confident SmartCentres has enough aces up its sleeve to mitigate the impact.

It had been a few years since I looked at SmartCentres as I wasn’t a big fan of the relatively high payout ratio for its dividend. The payout ratio remains high, but the distributions are covered while I also like the REIT’s expansion plans and the recent announcement by Walmart to expand its operations in Canada. I think it's reasonable to assume SmartCentres will be able to benefit from this, as it is already one of Walmart’s main landlords in Canada. And as SmartCentres was recently able to sign up Costco (COST) as a tenant, perhaps it can build out a successful relationship with them as well.

I currently have no position in SmartCentres, but as I'm increasing my exposure to Canadian REITs, I will definitely keep an eye on SmartCentres as an income idea.
