data.title: TSMC Q1: Excellent Results, Strong Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Article Thesis

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) aka TSMC reported excellent quarterly earnings results for the first quarter, showing it continues to benefit from broad AI tailwinds and its strong market position. The outlook for the current quarter is very positive as well and way better than what Wall Street was forecasting. Add an undemanding valuation, and this high-growth company looks like a Strong Buy – despite the geopolitical risks.

Past Coverage

I have written about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in January, around one quarter ago. I was bullish on TSMC back then – with the stock pulling back since then due to the broad market decline and with TSM reporting very strong results for the first quarter, I am even more bullish today.

What Happened?

On Thursday morning, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company reported its first-quarter earnings results. The headline numbers can be seen in the following screencap from Seeking Alpha:

We see that Taiwan Semiconductor did not deliver a double beat this time, but results still looked pretty good – the earnings per share consensus estimate was beaten by 3%, while the revenue miss was smaller than 1%. For a company that grows its revenues by way above 30% in US Dollars (growth was even higher in Taiwan's currency), I do not believe that a sub-1% revenue miss is very telling. Earnings per share are more important anyway – the bottom line counts, after all – and here the beat was relatively pronounced.

Markets reacted positively to these results, with TSMC trading up by around 3% at the time of writing. They could be volatile in the near term, however.

TSMC: Strong Results, Tariffs Don't Seem To Be A Threat

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is the leading foundry on the globe; thus, it is not surprising that it benefits when chip markets are strong. That undoubtedly is the case, mainly thanks to the very strong demand that AI chip companies such as Nvidia (NVDA) experience. Nvidia is one of TSMC's core customers – TSMC thus benefits a lot when Nvidia is seeing strong demand and rising sales.

While AI is a huge demand driver for TSM, the company also benefits from other macro themes. The company also experienced appealing growth in some non-AI areas, such as automotive. With automobiles becoming ever more "techy," demand for computing power rises – and with autonomous driving tech still in the early innings, there is a lot of potential for future growth in this area, I believe.

On a quarter-to-quarter basis, TSMC saw its revenues decline in the smartphone market, but that isn't a surprise – for smartphones and other consumer goods, the fourth quarter is especially strong, due to the holiday impact in that quarter. When we look at the Q4-to-Q1 performance, the comparison for these consumer-centric markets is thus a tough one, which explains why TSMC saw its smartphone revenues decline. The same can be expected in all future years – Q1 will almost always be weaker than the previous Q4 when it comes to smartphone revenues.

While a strong performance for the first quarter wasn't too surprising – the company gives monthly sales results, after all – the company's Q2 outlook was a major surprise. Many feared that the current tariffs and trade tensions would hurt Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, but that does not seem to be the case:

The company guides for $28.4 billion to $29.2 billion in revenue for the current quarter, with a guidance midpoint of $28.8 billion. The company did $20.6 billion in revenue in last year's second quarter, which means that the current guidance implies a revenue growth rate of 39.8%. That is not only better than the growth rate during the first quarter, but it also is a very high growth rate in general for the company – this doesn't suggest that TSMC is feeling a lot of pressure from tariffs. When we consider that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company tends to outperform its own guidance – it did so in the first quarter as well, beating it by $130 million – there is, I believe, a solid chance that the actual growth rate will surpass 40%. But even if that is not the case, a high-30s growth rate for a company as large as TSMC is outstanding.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's gross margin stood at 58.8%, which was above the guidance midpoint (TSMC tends to be conservative, as mentioned above) and which is highly attractive in absolute terms. TSMC's high gross margin, despite a manufacturing-heavy business model, is the result of strong pricing power. After all, as the global leader in its industry, TSMC can set prices that its customers basically have to take if they want advanced chips. For the current quarter, TSMC guides for a gross margin of around 58% – but that was the guidance for Q1 as well, and the company performed better, so I wouldn't be surprised if the same happens this quarter.

Looking at TSMC's cash flow generation, things were good during the first quarter as well. The company generated operating cash flows of around NT$626 billion, which is equal to a little more than $19 billion. Annualized, that is close to $80 billion, which is not only very impressive in absolute terms, but which is also massive for a company that is currently valued at "just" $680 billion. After all, if the rest of the year looks like Q1, TSMC would trade at an operating cash flow multiple of less than 9, or an operating cash flow yield of more than 11%. The company continues to invest heavily in its operations, of course, in order to benefit from the ongoing strong market growth rate in AI chips, but even after accounting for around $10 billion of capital expenditures, TSMC's free cash flows looked quite good, at around $9 billion – or around $36 billion annualized, which results in a free cash flow yield of well north of 5% despite TSMC's heavy investments.

TSMC: Undemanding Valuation, Some Macro Risks

Based on forecasted earnings per share of $9.07 during the current year, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company trades for 16.7x forward net profits. For a very fast-growing company that is an industry leader, that is not a high valuation at all, I believe. When we consider that TSMC beat the Q1 consensus and that it has a good chance of beating the Q2-Q4 consensus estimates as well – its guidance for Q2 was way stronger than expected, and it has a history of outperforming expectations, with more than 20 earnings per share beats in a row – its profits during the current year could easily come in north of $9.07, which would naturally mean that its "true" earnings multiple would be even lower than 16.7.

TSMC also looks quite inexpensive based on its enterprise value to EBITDA multiple:

The EV/EBITDA ratio, which accounts for debt and cash on the balance sheet, stands at just 9.7 right now – the lowest in recent memory, and way below the 5-year median at 14.1. To me, this suggests that TSMC is both quite cheap in absolute terms and quite cheap compared to how this high-quality growth player was valued in the past.

There are some geopolitical risks, mainly the potential for an escalation in the Taiwan conflict, but judging by TSMC's guidance, trade tensions and tariffs do not seem to be a problem, so the recent sell-off seems way overdone. For those that can stomach the Taiwan risk (which is, I believe, existent, but not overly large), TSMC looks very attractive right here – the company combined excellent growth, a strong outlook, a very compelling industry position, and a very undemanding valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HubSpot: Fundamentals Are Behind Its Valuation
data.description:
Despite strong revenue growth in the past, I expect Hubspot (NYSE:HUBS) to experience a stagnation in growth and be challenged against the market leader in customer relationship management - Salesforce (CRM), which boasts significantly larger revenue, market cap, and higher-value enterprise clients. Hubspot's focus on the SMB [small & medium business] market and reliance on acquisitions for growth are diminishing the future return on investment and profitability potential for the company.

Ultimately, I rate the company a “hold” as it has downside protection as a potential acquisition target from Salesforce competitors.

A possible reversal tailwind to keep track of is a rejuvenation of the SMB economy that was severely pressured in the last few years.

Hubspot vs Salesforce: It Pays To Be A Leader

Salesforce has 14x more revenues than Hubspot, trades at 9x the market cap compared to HUBS, has the leading market share, and more valuable clientele [higher-margin enterprise customers] versus HUBS.

Relative to sales, HUBS is also more expensive than CRM as it trades at 8.7x EV/Revenue [forward CY’25], compared to CRM’s 6x. However, the confounding factor here is that HUBS is estimated to grow at close to double the rate of CRM [and keep it up for the next few years], while CRM is expected to recover and maintain revenue growth rates around 10%.

Metric

HUBS

CRM

Revenue fwd. CY’25

$3B

$40.8B

Est. revenue growth

14.3%

7.8%

Market cap

$27.5B

$244.6B

+ Debt

$0.754B

$12B

- Cash

$2.07

$14.03B

= Enterprise Value

$26.2B

$242.6B

EV/Revenue (FWD)

8.7x

5.95x

Despite HUBS being able to outgrow CRM in the next few years, my view is that the type of customers CRM has [enterprise-level companies] have larger purchasing power and offer investors larger monetization potential. Hubspot has put itself in a tighter corner as its customers are primarily SMB which have more limited budgets, and can expand their spend based on their prospective growth levels. In essence, betting on HUBS is betting on the recovery of the economy for smaller businesses. Thus far, this category has been severely punished by the market as the value has accrued to the larger companies that keep consolidating the industry.

This is not to say that HUBS is a bad investment, but that the KPI for investors keeping HUBS in their portfolio should be the potential rejuvenation of the SMB and mid-market sector where the company holds some 3.4% market share. It is also a diversification component as CRM does not [and should not] bother capturing this market segment, hence making HUBS an addition to a portfolio that wants exposure to the software customer relationship market.

How Big Is The Customer Relationship Market

The customer relationship market size is estimated [1, 2, 3] to be between $76B and $101B, with a 2024 midpoint estimate of $89B. From this, CRM holds some 43%, and HUBS has about 3.4%. Peers in the space include Salesforce, Hubspot, Adobe [Marketo], Intuit’s MailChimp, Pipedrive, ActiveCampaign, Zoho, etc. The exuberance cycle in customer relationship management is slowly stabilizing and investors are now able to more reliably forecast growth, leading to a re-rating in equity values. The industry kicked-off with CRM as the pioneer, and had many startups that attempted to carve out a market slice in the last 10-years. So much so, that in my view, every other startup pitch was about creating a CRM software of some sort. However, we now see that trend slowing down as established players have captured key customers and built their feature moat. For this reason, I believe that the industry is entering its mature growth stage, and going forward I will be forecasting growth rates up to 15% for peers in this space.

A subsection of this market is known as customer engagement market [CEM], i.e. marketing automation and lead generation. This is HUBS’s key segment, where the company competes directly and has an 8% share of the estimated $38.4B total market. Separating these CRM and CEM markets is blurry, as many of the products companies offer lay somewhere in the customer relationship chain. So it will not be a stretch for peers to develop features that are used in different phases of the sales cycle.

Growth Vitals Are Stagnating

Thus far, HUBS’s operating profit has been negative. This is understandable as the company is engaged in growth investments. However, it is somewhat unsettling that the kind of growth levers the company is pulling aren’t purely organic, but it has leapt forward in its development cycle and moved to growth via acquisitions.

Hubspot has spent $40M for acquiring Cacheflow and $142M for APIHub [p. 81] in 2024 and 2023 respectively; and this year, HUBS spent $60M to acquire Frame AI [p. 91]. Typically, I view acquisitions as a great way to drive growth for companies that are at a mature growth stage, but are a risky play when the business itself is unprofitable. Growth via acquisitions indicates a lack of technological investment opportunities, and means that the company can’t entice market participants to switch to their products via superiority, but must buy out smaller peers in order to capture a market share large enough to build a stable moat.

Additionally, Hubspot’s average subscription revenue per customer is stagnating, which indicates that the company is likely pricing its products high, leading to smaller competitors recognising the opportunity to move in. Existing customers seem to be favoring lower-end subscriptions [p. 51] as they mitigate their SaaS spend. During my research of HUBS, the general sentiment from people reviewing the product is that HUBS is steeply priced with the pricing reflecting a lack of viable alternatives for customers, not the value they feel they get for features.

Essentially, HUBS now has to fend off disruptors, and is left with growing revenue by focusing on increasing the number of customers. For this reason, I think that growth vitals are showings signs of stagnation, and will be forecasting a decelerating growth rate in my modeling – as opposed to analysts which estimate that growth picks up after 2025.

The Profitability Question

After years of operating in the red, HUBS is on the cusp of breaking even at the business level. For FY’24 GAAP operating margin was -2.6%, compared to -9.3% in 2023. Non-GAAP operating margin was 17.5%, compared to 15.5% in 2023. The company has a non-GAAP op. margin target of 20% to 22% by 2027, indicating that GAAP profitability will likely be lower.

Investing in HUBS carries with it the implied assumption that the company will be able to execute on profitability once growth stabilizes.

In my view, the company has a longer road to achieving the profitability margins it has set in its earnings presentation and investors will be footing the stock-based compensation dilution for a significant period before seeing a return on their investment. This isn’t necessarily bad, it just means that investors entering the company at this stage are seeding their investment, but need to be prepared to wait for some time before the reaping phase arrives.

Hubspot Is Still Richly Priced

The 8.7x forward EV to revenue multiple is breaking below historical levels, which is a good sign if you believe the company can maintain double digit growth rates.

In my view, the company is about to break into profitability which will be a positive catalyst for the stock, but I also expect it to re-rate lower gradually as the market realizes that the high revenue growth rates may not be sustainable.

My target multiple for Hubspot is 5x EV/Revenue by 2028 [end of 2027], for which I project 14%, 12%, and 10% revenue growth rates respectively, leading to a forward revenue of $3.7B and an enterprise value of $18.5B.

The company has good cash balance management, which has led to $2B in reserves, of which I will net out $1B in order to reconcile EV to market cap. This leads to my estimated market value of $17.5B. HUBS has a diluted share count of 51.2M, which I expect to grow at a 4.3% CAGR, leading to a 58M share count by 2028. This leads to a forward pricing of $300 per share, significantly lower than the current $548 price. Discounting the forward pricing back to today at a 8% cost of equity, I get a present value of $14B or $240 per share.

Conversely, if you retain the assumption that the company should trade at 9x revenues, then the resulting forward value would be $33.3B or $574 per share. Discounted at a 8% rate, it implies a pricing of $456 per share or $26B, roughly in-line with today’s price. In a sense, this is the current market model which investors can use as a comparison to their forecasts.

In situations like this, where a stock is priced ahead of its value, I would typically estimate the time it takes for the fundamentals to converge to value. However, in the case of HUBS I view the R/R asymmetry to be negative i.e. I expect more bad news than good over the next three years; and would rather wait for a better opening.

For the reasons above I rate the company as a “hold”, with the assumption that the market re-rates down to CRM’s multiples instead of holding the elevated pricing.

Acquisition Potential In A Downturn

In the event of a negative market catalyst, HUBS management may find itself trading as a value stock which would open the conversation of integrating HUBS within a competitor like MSFT, ORCL, or even CRM. For this reason, I feel that investors have some downside protection as the business would continue to be valuable even if negative headwinds stack up. In my view, selling the company while you are ahead is a much better position to be in, but no-one can really tell how things will turn in the future.

Note that this has to be a willing decision, as investors may find it difficult to force an event like this because of the company’s protective bylaw structure [p. 39, 2].

Risks

  • The recent drop in pricing may look like an opportunity, but even if so, it is likely still behind the fundamentals.
  • AI and industry consolidation may pose a risk to the amount of new digital services the company can pitch to customers. While AI is a convenient tool for search and text summarization; customer service AI may cheapen a product suite.
  • While its digital products have a lower pricing than Salesforce, they may not be as sticky as management would hope.

data.title: Capitalizing On Biotech Market Shifts
data.description:
Join ROTY Biotech today!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone, Daniel Snyder here from Seeking Alpha. Today, we are going to be diving into the world of biotech, which I'm super excited about, especially within these crazy markets. I mean, obviously, as we're hosting this right now, April 16th, we saw Powell come out. There has been a quite - a move in the market right now. And well, that just seems to be the nature of things nowadays.

So obviously, when we focus on growth, we talk about biotech. We have Jonathan Faison from ROTY Biotech here today to talk to us. But first, let me go ahead and get a quick legal disclaimer out of the way.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Any views or opinions expressed in the webinar do not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or your financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, U.S. investment adviser, or investment bank.

Information provided by the investing group does not constitute investing advice. Investing group leaders are third-party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Any content and tools on the platform are offered for information purposes only.

And with that out of the way, I would love to introduce to you all Jonathan. If you don't know who he is, well, now you do. He is the man that I'm like, if I have a question about biotech, if I have something about FDA, if I have a question about what drug might be cornering a market or what's the next potential, he is the one to come to. So I'm super excited that we were able to grab some time from him today.

So Jonathan, how are you doing?

Jonathan Faison: Hey, good. Good to be here. Thank you, again.

DS: All right. So we've got a good sizable crowd here hanging out with us today. And I'm sure there's going to be questions for you about this space because it's some sort of an unknown. Some people don't even like to touch biotech, right, when it comes to…

JF: For good reason, I completely understand it.

DS: So before we dive deeper into talking about the stocks that you want to tell people about today and talking about the lay of the land, why don't you go ahead and just give people a kind of a quick background of how you got into all of this?

JF: Okay. So I think most who follow know the story, but I started investing in 2008, had no idea what I was doing. This was right before the financial crisis, probably lost half of the life savings into it, made a bunch of poor calls along the way, every mistake you can imagine, started writing on Seeking Alpha, basically to share my ideas, to keep improving.

One thing I loved is that the comment section was brutal, especially when it comes to biotech. Doctors and others who obviously have a lot more background knowledge than I do in the sector were able to call me out where I got it wrong. That's how you get better as an investor. I never thought it would turn into a biotech service of any kind. I was just sharing what I was doing with my portfolio, what's worked along the years, very lucky to have survived my mistakes and tried to share what I've learned so other people don't have to go through the same roller coaster ride that I did. Though, as you said with Powell lately, there's kind of no avoiding that.

DS: I mean, it's a little bit of everything, right? You get the overall market dynamics. But what I like is that you dive into these actual companies. And we had a lot of conversations the other week as we were planning for this about what we wanted to talk about and something you mentioned to me at that moment in time that I hope you can address here, as you say, it's kind of not even a fundamental market right now. Could you elaborate more into that?

JF: Sure. So on the slides, I don't know if we bring them up, but it doesn't. Sure. But anyway, they have the XBI, you can see it's fallen from 105 down to 70 over, even this the last half year, the BBC, which is a clinical biotech ETF, that's also at lows, it’s not even when we thought it was double bottoming at the same levels as the 2023 lows, it completely plummeted below that as well. And we're seeing that spread to – throughout the sector, the baby being thrown with – thrown out with the bathwater.

If we go down to the third slide, let me pull this up. All right, I guess you're controlling it. There we go. Yeah, you can see the sector, the BBC is down 46% over the last three years. And it's no wonder people are steering clear of the sector. If we move to the next one.

So the perfect storm continues. You've had funds that are forced to sell at exactly the moment they don't want to. Investor redemptions forced. You have liquidations of companies trading below cash. Everyone's asking is the sector broken or is it just a bear market? Personally, to me, that sounds like capitulation.

You could say almost 50%, almost half of companies under $1 billion are trading under negative enterprise value, trading below their cash. There's this issue of zombie biotech companies, which also turns people off to the sector, completely get it. These companies where their lead drug fails, their next drugs in the pipeline, let's say, are preclinical or they have nothing and they'll in-license something preclinical. And instead of being good stewards of capital and the best option would be to return that money to shareholders and close down. Sutro Biopharma is a good example, STRO, where we talked about it in the last webinar. I wrote an article on it how I liked the technology, but I couldn't get behind the company because their lead construct was going after folate receptor alpha, which is a crowded target in the ADC world going after - they tried to claim management that there was white space in the low to medium expressors of the target.

But actually, NCCN guidelines, the approved drug from ImmunoGen, which was a holding of ours, that got bought out by AbbVie, I believe. They actually can address those populations with the Avastin combination. And so Sutro, long story short, after all this time of claiming that they were going to continue with this construct, they let the news loose that they are de-prioritizing, which means they're going to try to partner. Probably nobody wants that asset, and they're going to move forward some preclinical drug candidates instead.

And so the market, you're not looking at any meaningful catalysts then for the company potentially until those. You have to get them through IND-enabling studies, then you have to get them in Phase 1, slow dose escalation up. And so long story short, there’s a lot of people who would argue that the company is better off liquidating and returning the cash to shareholders.

Fortunately, in the news this morning, we saw a company – a couple of companies where funds are pushing to do that. And then, of course, the uncertainty from the Trump layoffs at the FDA, a lot of companies are asking whether the timelines are still intact for regulatory, if they've agreed on endpoints for certain studies, especially in the rare disease area where if we're waiting for longer-term outcomes, these studies could take forever.

So they're allowing for biomarker endpoints. Companies are wondering if that's still on the table, especially with Marks gone as well. So definitely, the uncertainty there as we were talking about Powell’s remarks today, unfortunately, there's this connotation that biotech – when the overall market is up, we're up more, but when it's down, we're down even more as well. And so it's definitely the highest volatility, highest risk sector.

DS: Yeah, I was going to mention on top of that, I mean, I was just pulling up XBI here, year-to-date down 17% for the sector from the XBI ETF. So some people look at this market environment. We've been talking about this lately in webinars that I'm sure the market's crazy, but there's actually these green shoots of opportunities.

JF: Sure.

DS: And even Steve was talking about the Quant system the other day, like one of the first Mag7 stocks has actually populated as a Strong Buy. And that was Amazon, if you wanted to go back and watch it. But we're starting to see the prices of stocks come down. So now we're all turned to the earnings and waiting for the earnings calls to come down the line and see what happens with management and forward guidance and everything else that everybody's worried about right now. How is that impacting biotech in general? I mean, they've…

JF: Sure.

DS: …their Capex – they spend, right? They spend, they go take the money for the loans. They're trying to do all these medical drugs, there's a lot of uncertainty. How are we supposed to invest here in this sector right now?

JF: Sure, if we can move to the next slide, and a shout out, I see Galzus Research in the comments as well for those who perhaps don't follow him. He does a lot of public articles on Seeking Alpha. I'm a big fan. I found him to be rather balanced. Balanced takes are hard to come by in biotech.

As far as you were saying, definitely in my case in the portfolio, we have about 70% of the portfolio focused on commercial-stage biotechs. The reason is those have been holding up well. Those have been kind of doing the heavy lifting in my portfolio the past couple of years. That's why we've been doing all right. And 30% is the maximum I'm allowed to invest in clinical-stage companies. And that's simply because those have been, as you said, the area that's been hit the hardest.

The flip side is crisis is their opportunity. It is a stock pickers market. The National Security Commission on Emerging Biotechnology was a bipartisan committee came out with strongly worded support for the sector. The guidelines they gave were pretty interesting about prioritizing the U.S. biotech, how we don't want to fall behind the Chinese sector or Chinese competition. We need to encourage innovation, manufacturing, get support, build the workforce. And so a lot of the guidelines they are suggesting are actually bullish for the sector long-term.

So one of the things I say is, right now, biotech is no offense, the redheaded stepchild of the stock market. And what happens if the opposite comes true in the coming years where they actually prioritize the sector. And that would be – we go from the place everybody's steering clear from to the place where everybody wants to invest in.

As far as how to navigate the current market, the bright point for me, I've been seeing lately is growth investors. People like you said, who normally say, I don't want to even touch biotech, cash burning companies, but they are finding these growth-oriented commercial-stage biotech companies, they’re all about revenue growth year-over-year. They're launching new drugs in exciting spaces.

And so for me personally, the sweet spot has been finding those companies when they're trading around enterprise value of 1x conservative peak sales or finding companies that are already have Phase 3 data in hand or they're already in regulatory submission, and we try to invest in them before they become growth stocks.

DS: So what about like, how are tariffs going to be affecting these companies? Because I know some of them deal abroad, whether it's clinical trials or even trying to, I don't know, get simple things like needles and syringes, right? For example, like all the tariffs that are coming through to medical supplies, how are these companies going to be navigating this new environment?

JF: So what's fun is every month or so we have a coffee at the close, which is all the members of ROTY Biotech Community, veterans and newbies. We're all sharing our thoughts on the sector and tariffs. The macro scene is definitely not my forte. So it was nice to hear from others who are more well versed on it, particularly Tom, TDM Invests. He is a member who actually works in the wine industry where the margins are pretty poor. He would be the first to admit.

One thing he likes about biotech is it's a high-margin industry for these drugs, once you actually get them approved and marketed. And so, we definitely underestimated the impact of these tariffs, at least, to sentiment for the sector.

But on the whole, a lot of these companies, especially those with U.S.-based manufacturing are saying, coming out with statements that they would not be affected to a large degree by these tariffs. But again, it’s – biotech is driven by sentiment as well, and we've seen what that's done to sentiment so far.

On the flip side, it's been nice that Trump, the executive order today, that he came out with had – they were targeting the PBMs, the middlemen. That's nice as opposed to going after – purely just after the biotech companies when it comes to drug pricing. The Inflation Reduction Act or IRA penalizes small molecule drug developers seven years before price negotiations can take place versus 11 years for biologics.

So it looks like they're going to try to tackle that as well. So there's definitely some, as you said, green shoots, but one thing is talk and another thing is action and we have to wait and see.

DS: There's an interesting question I want to go ahead and slip in here too, because you had mentioned with FDA, there's the whole shakeup at the FDA and Peter is here joining us today. He says, is there any evidence of corruption kind of payouts to get the FDA approvals in this space during this time? Do you have any thoughts on that?

JF: I will use the example of Agenus biotech, A-G-E-N, AGEN who we had it as a portfolio holding way back in the day when they were trying to get their first approval for Bal and Zali, their CTLA-4 and their PD-1 and cervical cancer.

And they were ahead of the competition for that second-line indication, I believe it was – was it Merck who was behind them with KEYTRUDA and the FDA leapfrogged Merck, got them approved, which means that pathway, the accelerated approval was now blocked for Agenus.

So I'm not saying there's corruption, but it does seem like there are instances where big pharma is incentivized or favored over small biotech companies. And you remember biotech is a minefield to begin with, so many things that can go wrong, whether from clinical trial holds, a serious adverse event that otherwise tanks a safe drug, all these different variables.

And so if you're an investor and you feel like you've gotten your thesis right and something like this comes out of left field, I can see how it definitely discourages people.

DS: Yeah. I mean, there – and there's also constant shakeup, right? I think what we've all seen from this administration is they're moving extremely fast. People don't feel like they can catch up. They don't feel like they can anticipate what's going to happen next. So how are these companies going to be able to even plan for the future of how they're going to operate during all these trials and tribulations ahead for them? I can't help but think of maybe you have some opportunities and some ideas of solid companies. But before we dive into those, I wanted to ask you about AI, and how AI is affecting the sector right now. So before asking any further questions, just would you mind giving your overview of how AI is either benefiting or hurting biotech?

JF: Sure. Before I get there, your last question, just so I don't let it go by, reminds me of a basketball movie. I mentioned this analogy before, this metaphor, where the coach holds the basketball and says, this ball represents everything there is to know about the game. And then with a Sharpie he puts one point on it and says, this is what you know about it. And that's how I feel about biotech.

There's so much I don't know. And so when it comes to these companies and you're asking how are they going to survive, that's a great question. There's a lot of, I like to call them science projects out there where it seems like they're throwing everything at the wall with their technology, seeing what works and we try to steer clear of those companies.

So there's a large portion of the biotech universe that perhaps is uninvestable. And as Galzus said in the comments, it's a stock picker's market. So for me, I tried to focus just on the areas where there are higher probability setups, whether that's commercial stage or these are companies that already have a clear path to market. So they're not a science project. They actually have a legitimate commercial opportunity, they're addressing a high unmet need, they're either going after an area where there is no competition or where the data they have in hand is markedly better than the competition.

So you try to handicap it as much as possible in your favor, whatever your niche is fine, whatever works for you. TDM Invests in our chat, he likes to invest in what he calls the black sheep of biotech, which are basically unloved commercial companies, companies that everybody loves to hate. And maybe before they were high flyers, but at a certain point when they come down so low that everybody hates them, he's not looking for a home run. He's just looking for sentiment to improve a little bit or for this company to execute. It doesn't have to be a home run out of the park. So find whatever works for you and stick with it.

As far as AI goes, that's a great question. I think we're in the really early innings of it. I won't claim to be an expert on that space, but we did see how AI was used to accelerate the vaccine development for the COVID and so forth.

And so we're already seeing companies that are focused on that for drug development. There was recent news that they're trying to eventually phase out animal testing from biotech and they would use AI modules instead. Again, that's going to take quite some time. We don't have any timeline for that, but that would be really interesting.

DS: So for somebody like me, I don't know biotech that well. But when you're saying that they're using AI within the biotech space, is it more of kind of creating a fake clinical run of a test just digitally across many tests? Like, what are they doing specifically with AI to help streamline and become more efficient or develop these new drugs?

JF: Well, I was fortunate when I was in Panama, for some reason, a lot of the AI entrepreneurs are there, ran into some heads of companies there. And they talked not only about biotech, but every sector, how they plan to revolutionize it. It's almost like a religion.

And when it comes to biotech, I think, they're in the early innings still of figuring it out, but the idea, and I wish I had a graph in front of me, I don't, of the failure rate. There's a very high failure rate from preclinical to Phase 1, from Phase 1 to Phase 2, et cetera. And so the idea would be to leverage AI to not only accelerate the pace of drug development, but also to reduce – to increase the success rate as much as possible, reduce that failure rate. So that would be really interesting to see, but again, not my forte.

DS: Got you. There's an interesting question here about utilizing synthetic DNA and what it means for drug discovery. Do you have any thoughts on that?

JF: Quite a few of the questions I'm seeing, I'll put it this way, I see questions about companies that are not in our portfolio. And it's fun in our chat when you have 500 people, the nice thing is, out of any name, there's going to be a few people who own it and who can discuss it.

So for me personally, I don't have that kind of attention span to be able to follow lots of companies. In the portfolio, we probably own anywhere from 10, maximum 20, usually around 15 is the sweet spot companies. And so I'll be the first to say when either there's a ticker I'm not familiar with or I'm vaguely familiar with it, but not knowledgeable enough to provide my opinion on it. So the same goes with that topic.

DS: Yeah, man, appreciate the honesty. I know we all do. But speaking of your portfolio, last time we talked – you talked about you actually kind of shifted the way you approach your portfolio more recently. Would you mind just sharing that with the changes you made?

JF: Oh, sure. So that was 2023, again, where we made the transition to focusing on commercial stage biotech. Over time, I limited the clinical stage portion of the portfolio to only 30% as a maximum. And the reason is, I just found out not only am I avoiding the early stage, but for the later-stage clinical, it just hasn't had the same hit rate, the same success in this environment as commercial.

And so put it this way, 2023 was the first year we made that transition and the portfolio returned 117%. Last year, we were up around 45% into Q4. And then some binary catalysts, completely my own fault. I was holding size and clinical stage companies that were too risky, lost quite a few of those gains, ended up down – up just 14% for the year. And that was a reminder to me, for commercial stage positions, the maximum I'm allowed to own no matter how much I love the company is 10% of the portfolio.

So if the drug launch goes well, we do well, great. And for clinical stage biotech, the maximum I'm allowed to own is 5% of the portfolio. So if it does really well, you get that elusive 5 or 10 bagger, that would be amazing. And if it doesn't, you're going to live and survive to fight another day.

I think so much of the time, especially I did when I started out, I forgot, or I didn't realize it's all about risk management and developing not only a resilient portfolio, but resilient personal finances. So many times investors, we focus on the balance sheets of these companies, but not our own personal balance sheet.

The idea, obviously, we're not Buffett or anybody, but we want to be doing well or all right, staying in the game when the market's doing what it's doing now, when it's a really poor period and able to each month put money to work in high-quality names.

DS: Yeah, duly noted. So shifting gears here, I want to ask you maybe one or two more questions before we dive into some stocks that you wanted to talk about here today that have what you say opportunity, right?

JF: Sure.

DS: First up, what happened to the M&A space? What's going on with M&A within biotech right now?

JF: That's a great question. It seems to have evaporated. It was a couple of years ago, we had some good ones, Orchard, Immunogen. At the beginning of this year, there was ITCI. I did not own it. And from there, it's been pretty quiet. This one is not in the slides. It wasn't a company I was going to mention to those watching, but SpringWorks Therapeutics, SWTX.
